text: Three-D augmented reality robotic assisted segmental ureterectomy with buccal mucosal graft for the repair of upper complex ureteral strictures. Ureteral stenosis constitutes a very complex surgical scenario with a high rate of postoperative failure. For two to three centimeters upper strictures, a robotic-assisted segmental ureteral resection with subsequent uretero-ureteral anastomosis is considered a valid treatment option. A buccal mucosa graft can be used to create the anterior plate of the anastomosis with the aim of reducing the tension of the suture.

In order to intraoperatively locate the stenosis, a concomitant ureteroscopy is usually employed. However, the addition of the endoscopy increases the intraoperative complexity and costs of the surgery and may lead to iatrogenic damage to the ureter, increasing the risk of a post-surgical treatment failure. Among the alternative strategies, ICG and the luminal injection has been proposed as an intraoperative aid to identify the stenosis borders, but it needs a nephrostomium site as well as the intraoperative ureteroscopy employment. Three-D virtual models have been also used as a guide to locate the stricture in a cognitive way intraoperatively.

In order to increase the precision in identifying the stricture during the surgical dissection of the ureter, a step further to the use of cognitive three-D guidance has to be done, testing the role of augmented reality surgical navigation. Such technology has already demonstrated great usefulness in identifying hidden targets in other surgical settings, such as prostate lesions, kidney neoplasms and iliac artery plaques in kidney transplant. In case of upper ureteral strictures not detectable on the organ surface, as it happens for stones embedded into the ureteral wall, the augmented reality technology can allow the precise identification of the target, minimizing the removal of peristenotic healthy tissue during the surgical dissection.

An assistant uses a three-D mouse to manually superimpose a three-D virtual model of the ureter onto the operative field, visible in real-time through the DaVinci Surgical Console's Tile Pro technology. The lower pole of the kidney and the intersection with iliac vessels serve as markers for precise overlay.

The video demonstrates three cases of ureteral strictures above the iliac vessels. In the first case, augmented reality is used to overlay a 3D model of the ureter and an embedded stone onto the operative field, allowing precise identification of the stricture. The surgeon incises and resects the stenotic ureteral tract, confirming accuracy with ureteroscopy. In the second case, an omental flap is prepared, and augmented reality aids in identifying and resecting the stricture in the middle portion of the ureter, sparing healthy tissue. The embedded stone is visible during resection.

In the third case, the surgeon identifies microcalcifications in the ureteral wall just below the ureteropoietic junction. Indocyanine green (ICG) is injected through nephrostomy, but it does not provide precise feedback on the stricture level. The ureter is then cut and spatulated, preparing for the reconstructive phase.

The surgeon prepares a buccal mucosal graft by contouring a 3x1 cm section of oral mucosa and cutting it with forceps. The graft is placed into the abdominal cavity after the posterior plate of the ureteral anastomosis is completed with a terminal-terminal suture. The graft is applied over the ureteral defect and sutured to the peripheral margins, modeling the graft tissue to repair the defect and restore the ureteral caliber for instrument progression.

The surgeon wraps the previously prepared omental flap around the repaired ureter to ensure vascularization of the graft, aiming to prevent stricture recurrence. The surgical procedure concludes successfully without significant complications.

Augmented reality intraoperative guidance showed to be a useful and precise technological tool able to correctly identify the ureteral stricture even when not visible on the ureteral wall surface, leading to maximize the preservation of the ureteral peristrictural healthy tissue.
